text: The robotic multivisceral resection involves stapling off the small bowel, which is visibly involved with the T4 N1 tumor, and leaving it attached to the specimen.

The DaVinci X robotic system is utilized to perform the division of the small bowel using a Shoreform X-T stapler with a blue cartridge. The robotic vessel sealer is employed to divide the mesentery of the small bowel, followed by the division of the efferent loop with a second stapler firing.

The surgeon begins dissection at the sacral promontory, identifying the iliac vessels, ureter, and inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). Dissection proceeds cranially from below the IMA towards its origin from the aorta, using Endo-wrist instruments for enhanced visualization and safe manipulation around the sympathetic nerves.
The surgeon skeletonizes the artery, applies Hem-o-lok clips using the assistant port on the right side, and then divides the artery.

The surgeon focuses on the inferior mesenteric vein near the DJ flexure, defining the anatomy in this area. A medial to lateral dissection is performed to separate the colonic mesentery from Girota's fascia, with the assistant providing counter-direction on the paranephric fat.

The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) after placing hemlock clips, performing a high division under the pancreas. The dissection continues cranially to separate the colon and access the lesser sac anterior to the pancreas.

The surgeon performs further medial to lateral dissection to separate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) from the gonadal vessels and left ureter. Visible inflammation and edema are present in the dissection plane due to cancer-related desmoplasia. The lateral release of the left colon and sigmoid is facilitated by traction and counter-traction, making the dissection straightforward.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing instrument to finalize the adhesion between the spleen and the colon, employing bipolar energy to securely divide vessels up to seven millimeters in size.

The surgeon focuses on the pelvis, performing posterior dissection in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane. The tumor involves the bladder, uterus, and small bowel anteriorly. Dissection proceeds in the pre-sacral space, revealing the right inferior hypogastric nerve at the five o'clock position. The bladder is visibly involved with cancer, and a full-thickness entry is made into the bladder, exposing a malignant fistula to the colon and the Foley catheter within the bladder lumen.

The surgeon uses a gas sealing device to divide the round ligament and ovarian pedicle on the right side. The uterus, which contains large fibroids, is dissected along with the bladder. A similar dissection is performed on the left side to mobilize the left half of the colon and divide the left tubo-ovarian pedicle, preparing for an en bloc hysterectomy.

The surgeon completes bladder dissection using a vessel sealing device, pulling the uterus upwards to develop a plane behind the bladder and uterus. Minor bleeding occurs, managed by an assistant using active suction. The specimen is mobilized cranially, creating space in the pelvis for mesorectal transection. A robotic stapler is used to transect the rectum with a single firing of a sixty shoreform stapler, releasing the specimen by dividing remaining adhesions.

The final view of the pelvis shows both ureters clearly visible. The vaginal vault is closed with a running 3-0 V-lock suture. The partial cystectomy has resulted in a transection line near the trigone, and both ureters are stented to prevent accidental capture during bladder suturing. The bladder is closed in two layers using a 3-0 V-lock suture, facilitated by Endo-wrist instruments.

The operating time for this case was three hundred minutes. The blood loss was fifty mils. An end colostomy was fashioned in the left iliac fossa because of uncertainty of complete clearance of the cancer given the advanced nature of her tumor. This colostomy can potentially be reversed in the near future if she remains tumor-free. Histology of the resected bowel was consistent with a T-IV-BN-Zero tumor of her rectosigmoid junction with complete R-Zero resection. Twenty-six lymph nodes were harvested and were clear. Patient was admitted to surgical high care unit for twenty four hours and then transferred to the ward where she stayed for four days.
